revision.comment: created article
revision.text:
'''Kuni no miyatsuko''' (国造 - also read ''Kokuso'' or ''Kuni no Miyakko'') were officials in [[ancient Japan]] at the time of the Yamato court. They were in charge of provinces (国), although it is not always very clear what those provinces were. Kuni no miyatsuko had most certainly vast powers and were local lords simply "appointed" by Yamato court. Kuni no miyatsuko as rulers were abolished during the [[Taika reforms]], and provinces were reorganized under the [[ritsuryo]] system. Kuni no miyatsuko were appointed [[gunshi]], whereas provinces became ruled by [[kokushi]]. The post remained after the Taika reforms, but the Kuni no miyatsuko were now in charge of spiritual and religious affairs. A few Kuni no miyatsuko clans however retained influence after the Taika reform, such as the Izumo Kuni no miyatsuko (出雲国造).
{{Japan-hist-stub}} [[category:Ancient Japan]]

revision.text:
{{Nihongo|'''Kuni no miyatsuko'''|国造|}}, also read as "kokuzō" or "'kuni tsu ko", were officials in [[ancient Japan]] at the time of the [[Yamato period|Yamato]] court.
==Yamato period==
Kuni no miyatsuko governed small territories ({{Nihongo|kuni|国|}}), although the location, names, and borders of the provinces remain unclear. Kuni no miyatsuko were appointed by and remained under the jurisdiction of the Yamato Court, but over time the position became heriditary.<ref name="dijitaru">{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru daijisen | title = 国造 (Kuni no miyatsuko) | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/| accessdate = 2012-04-12 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo}}{{ja icon}}</ref><ref name="encyclopedia">{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Encyclopedia of Japan | title = Kuni no miyatsuko | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-04-12 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo}}</ref> Kuni no miyatsuko carried [[kabane]] honoric names bestowed by the Yamato Court, commonly "kimi" or "atae". Prestigious Kuni no miyatsuko were called "omi".<ref name="encyclopedia"/>
==Taika Reform==
The office of kuni no miyatsuko was abolished in the [[Taika reforms]] in 645 and the former administrative ‘’kuni’’ provinces were formally reorganized under the [[ritsuryō]] system. The provinces became ruled by new officials called {{Nihongo|kuni no mikotomochi||}}, or more commonly, {{Nihongo|[[Kokushi (officials)|kokushi]]|国司}}.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> The kuni no miyatsuko continued to be appointed after the Taika Reform]], generally to the office of {{Nihongo|gunji|郡司|}}.<ref name="dijitaru"/> Gunji were appointed from powerful regional kuni no miyatsuko families, appointed for life, and the position became heriditary.<ref name="dijitaru-gunji">{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru daijisen | title = 郡司 (Gunji) | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/| accessdate = 2012-04-12 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo}}{{ja icon}}</ref>. The Kuni no miyatsuko were now in charge of spiritual and religious affairs, specifically the [[Shintō]] rites of each province.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> These religious officials became knowns as {{Nihongo|shin kokuzō|新国造|}}, or "new" kuni no miyatsuko.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> The kuni no miyatsuko, now in the office of gunji, often sided with peasants against the ruling kokushi elite. The gunji position, however, was abolished with the establishment of the shōen system in the early Heian period.<ref name="dijitaru-gunji"/> A few kuni no miyatsuko clans retained influence after the Taika reform, such as the {{Nihongo|Izumo no Kuni no miyatsuko|出雲国造}} of [[Izumo Province]] in present-day eastern [[Shimane Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) (日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) | title = Izumo Kuni no miyatsuko (出雲国造) | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/| accessdate = 2012-04-12 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo}}{{ja icon}}</ref>
==References==
{{Reflist}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:Kuni No Miyatsuko}} [[Category:Ancient Japan]]
{{Portal|Ancient Japan}} {{Japan-hist-stub}}
[[it:Kuni no miyatsuko]] [[ja:国造]] [[zh:國造]]

Kuni no miyatsuko governed small territories ({{Nihongo|kuni|国|}}), although the location, names, and borders of the provinces remain unclear. Kuni no miyatsuko were appointed by and remained under the jurisdiction of the Yamato Court, but over time the position became heriditary.<ref name="dijitaru">{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru daijisen | title = 国造 (Kuni no miyatsuko) | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/| accessdate = 2012-04-12 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo}}{{ja icon}}</ref><ref name="encyclopedia">{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Encyclopedia of Japan | title = Kuni no miyatsuko | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | accessdate = 2012-04-12 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo}}</ref> Kuni no miyatsuko carried [[kabane]] honoric names bestowed by the Yamato Court, commonly "kimi" or "atae". Prestigious Kuni no miyatsuko were called "omi".<ref name="encyclopedia"/>
==Taika Reform==
The office of kuni no miyatsuko was abolished in the [[Taika reforms]] in 645 and the former administrative ‘’kuni’’ provinces were formally reorganized under the [[ritsuryō]] system. The provinces became ruled by new officials called {{Nihongo|kuni no mikotomochi||}}, or more commonly, {{Nihongo|[[Kokushi (officials)|kokushi]]|国司}}.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> The kuni no miyatsuko continued to be appointed after the Taika Reform, generally to the office of {{Nihongo|gunji|郡司|}}.<ref name="dijitaru"/> Gunji were appointed from powerful regional kuni no miyatsuko families, appointed for life, and the position became heriditary.<ref name="dijitaru-gunji">{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Dijitaru daijisen | title = 郡司 (Gunji) | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/| accessdate = 2012-04-12 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo}}{{ja icon}}</ref> The Kuni no miyatsuko were now in charge of spiritual and religious affairs, specifically the [[Shintō]] rites of each province.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> These religious officials became known as {{Nihongo|shin-kokuzō|新国造|}}, or "new" kuni no miyatsuko.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> The kuni no miyatsuko, now in the office of gunji, often sided with peasants against the ruling kokushi elite. The gunji position, however, was abolished with the establishment of the shōen system in the early Heian period.<ref name="dijitaru-gunji"/> A few kuni no miyatsuko clans retained influence after the Taika reform, such as the {{Nihongo|Izumo no Kuni no miyatsuko|出雲国造}} of [[Izumo Province]] in present-day eastern [[Shimane Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) (日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) | title = Izumo Kuni no miyatsuko (出雲国造) | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/| accessdate = 2012-04-12 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo}}{{ja icon}}</ref>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Officials in ancient Japan during the Yamato period}} {{Nihongo|'''''Kuni no miyatsuko'''''|国造|}}, also read as '''''kokuzō''''' or '''''kunitsuko''''', were officials in [[ancient Japan]] during the [[Yamato period]] who governed provinces called ''kuni''.
==Yamato period==
''Kuni no miyatsuko'' governed provinces called ''kuni'' (国), although the location, names, and borders of the provinces remain unclear. ''Kuni no miyatsuko'' were appointed by and remained under the jurisdiction of the Yamato Court, but over time the position became hereditary.<ref name="dijitaru">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Dijitaru daijisen |title=国造 (Kuni no miyatsuko) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref><ref name="encyclopedia">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Japan |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref> ''Kuni no miyatsuko'' carried ''[[kabane]]'' titles bestowed by the Yamato Court, commonly [[Kimi (kabane)|''kimi'']] (君) or ''[[atae]]'' (直). Prestigious ''kuni no miyatsuko'' held the title of ''[[Omi (title)|omi]]'' (臣).<ref name="encyclopedia"/>
==Taika Reforms==
The office of ''kuni no miyatsuko'' was abolished in the [[Taika Reform]]s in 645 and the former administrative ''kuni'' (provinces) were formally reorganized under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. The provinces became ruled by new officials called ''kuni no mikotomochi'', or more commonly, [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']].<ref name="encyclopedia"/> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'' continued to be appointed after the Taika Reforms, generally to the office of {{Nihongo|gunji|郡司|}}.<ref name="dijitaru"/> ''Gunji'' were appointed from powerful regional ''kuni no miyatsuko'' families, for life, and the position became hereditary.<ref name="dijitaru-gunji">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Dijitaru daijisen |title=郡司 (Gunji) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'' were now in charge of spiritual and religious affairs, specifically the [[Shinto|Shintō]] rites of each province.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> These religious officials became known as {{Nihongo|shin-kokuzō|新国造|}}, or "new" ''kuni no miyatsuko''.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'', now in the office of ''gunji'', often sided with peasants against the ruling ''kokushi'' elite. The ''gunji'' position, however, was abolished with the establishment of the manorial ''[[shōen]]'' system in the early [[Heian period]].<ref name="dijitaru-gunji"/> A few ''kuni no miyatsuko'' clans retained influence after the Taika Reforms, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]|出雲国造}} of [[Izumo Province]] in present-day eastern [[Shimane Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) (日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) |title=Izumo Kuni no miyatsuko (出雲国造) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref>
== Legacy == After their abolition several former Kuni no Miyatsuko became prominent priestly families these include the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the [[Owari clan]] of [[Atsuta Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref> the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]<!--original source text from [[Kono Shrine]] *{{nihongo|'''Amabe clan genealogy'''|海部氏系図||}}, early [[Heian period]], it is considered the oldest family tree in Japan.The clan claims descent from Amenohoakari, and served as at the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of [[Tanba Province]] before it was divided into Tamba and Tango.The document records 82 generations of descent from Amenohoakari. It was designated a National Treasure in 1972.--><ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]. {| class="wikitable" |+ !Province !Clan !Shrine |- |[[Izumo Province]] |[[Izumo clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Izumo-taisha]] |- |[[Yamato Province]] |[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] |[[Ōyamato Shrine]] |- |[[Higo Province]]? |[[Aso clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Aso Shrine]] |- |[[Owari Province]] |[[Owari clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Atsuta Shrine]] |- |[[Tanba Province]] |[[Amabe clan]]<ref name="Bunka1" /> |[[Kono Shrine]] |- | rowspan="2" |[[Kii Province]] |Ukai family |[[Kamayama Shrine]] |- |{{ill|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} |[[Hinokuma Shrine]] |}

{{Short description|Officials in ancient Japan during the Yamato period}} {{Nihongo|'''''Kuni no miyatsuko'''''|国造|}}, also read as '''''kokuzō''''' or '''''kunitsuko''''', were officials in [[ancient Japan]] during the [[Yamato period]] who governed provinces called ''kuni''.
==Yamato period==
''Kuni no miyatsuko'' governed provinces called ''kuni'' (国), although the location, names, and borders of the provinces remain unclear. ''Kuni no miyatsuko'' were appointed by and remained under the jurisdiction of the Yamato Court, but over time the position became hereditary.<ref name="dijitaru">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Dijitaru daijisen |title=国造 (Kuni no miyatsuko) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref><ref name="encyclopedia">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Japan |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref> ''Kuni no miyatsuko'' carried ''[[kabane]]'' titles bestowed by the Yamato Court, commonly [[Kimi (kabane)|''kimi'']] (君) or ''[[atae]]'' (直). Prestigious ''kuni no miyatsuko'' held the title of ''[[Omi (title)|omi]]'' (臣).<ref name="encyclopedia"/>
==Taika Reforms==
The office of ''kuni no miyatsuko'' was abolished in the [[Taika Reform]]s in 645 and the former administrative ''kuni'' (provinces) were formally reorganized under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. The provinces became ruled by new officials called ''kuni no mikotomochi'', or more commonly, [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']].<ref name="encyclopedia"/> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'' continued to be appointed after the Taika Reforms, generally to the office of {{Nihongo|gunji|郡司|}}.<ref name="dijitaru"/> ''Gunji'' were appointed from powerful regional ''kuni no miyatsuko'' families, for life, and the position became hereditary.<ref name="dijitaru-gunji">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Dijitaru daijisen |title=郡司 (Gunji) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'' were now in charge of spiritual and religious affairs, specifically the [[Shinto|Shintō]] rites of each province.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> These religious officials became known as {{Nihongo|shin-kokuzō|新国造|}}, or "new" ''kuni no miyatsuko''.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'', now in the office of ''gunji'', often sided with peasants against the ruling ''kokushi'' elite. The ''gunji'' position, however, was abolished with the establishment of the manorial ''[[shōen]]'' system in the early [[Heian period]].<ref name="dijitaru-gunji"/> A few ''kuni no miyatsuko'' clans retained influence after the Taika Reforms, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]|出雲国造}} of [[Izumo Province]] in present-day eastern [[Shimane Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) (日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) |title=Izumo Kuni no miyatsuko (出雲国造) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref>
== Legacy == After their abolition several former Kuni no Miyatsuko became prominent priestly families these include the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the [[Owari clan]] of [[Atsuta Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref> the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]<!--original source text from [[Kono Shrine]] *{{nihongo|'''Amabe clan genealogy'''|海部氏系図||}}, early [[Heian period]], it is considered the oldest family tree in Japan.The clan claims descent from Amenohoakari, and served as at the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of [[Tanba Province]] before it was divided into Tamba and Tango.The document records 82 generations of descent from Amenohoakari. It was designated a National Treasure in 1972.--><ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]. {| class="wikitable" |+ !Province !Clan !Shrine |- |[[Izumo Province]] |[[Izumo clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Izumo-taisha]] |- |[[Yamato Province]] |[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] |[[Ōyamato Shrine]] |- |[[Higo Province]]? |[[Aso clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Aso Shrine]] |- |[[Owari Province]] |[[Owari clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Atsuta Shrine]] |- |[[Tanba Province]] |[[Amabe clan]]<ref name="Bunka1" /> |[[Kono Shrine]] |- | rowspan="2" |[[Kii Province]] |Ukai family |[[Kamayama Shrine]] |- |{{ill|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} |[[Hinokuma Shrine]] |}

It was designated a National Treasure in 1972.--><ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]. {| class="wikitable" |+ !Province !Clan !Shrine |- |[[Izumo Province]] |[[Izumo clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Izumo-taisha]] |- |[[Yamato Province]] |[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] |[[Ōyamato Shrine]] |- |[[Higo Province]]? |[[Aso clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Aso Shrine]] |- |[[Owari Province]] |[[Owari clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Atsuta Shrine]] |- |[[Tanba Province]] |[[Amabe clan]]<ref name="Bunka1" /> |[[Kono Shrine]] |- | rowspan="2" |[[Kii Province]] |Ukai family |[[Kamayama Shrine]] |- |{{ill|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} |[[Hinokuma Shrine]] |}

{{Short description|Officials in ancient Japan during the Yamato period}} {{Nihongo|'''''Kuni no miyatsuko'''''|国造|}}, also read as '''''kokuzō''''' or '''''kunitsuko''''', were officials in [[ancient Japan]] during the [[Yamato period]] who governed provinces called ''kuni''.
==Yamato period==
''Kuni no miyatsuko'' governed provinces called ''kuni'' (国), although the location, names, and borders of the provinces remain unclear. ''Kuni no miyatsuko'' were appointed by and remained under the jurisdiction of the Yamato Court, but over time the position became hereditary.<ref name="dijitaru">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Dijitaru daijisen |title=国造 (Kuni no miyatsuko) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref><ref name="encyclopedia">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Japan |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref> ''Kuni no miyatsuko'' carried ''[[kabane]]'' titles bestowed by the Yamato Court, commonly [[Kimi (kabane)|''kimi'']] (君) or ''[[atae]]'' (直). Prestigious ''kuni no miyatsuko'' held the title of ''[[Omi (title)|omi]]'' (臣).<ref name="encyclopedia"/>
==Taika Reforms==
The office of ''kuni no miyatsuko'' was abolished in the [[Taika Reform]]s in 645 and the former administrative ''kuni'' (provinces) were formally reorganized under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. The provinces became ruled by new officials called ''kuni no mikotomochi'', or more commonly, [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']].<ref name="encyclopedia"/> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'' continued to be appointed after the Taika Reforms, generally to the office of {{Nihongo|gunji|郡司|}}.<ref name="dijitaru"/> ''Gunji'' were appointed from powerful regional ''kuni no miyatsuko'' families, for life, and the position became hereditary.<ref name="dijitaru-gunji">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Dijitaru daijisen |title=郡司 (Gunji) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'' were now in charge of spiritual and religious affairs, specifically the [[Shinto|Shintō]] rites of each province.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> These religious officials became known as {{Nihongo|shin-kokuzō|新国造|}}, or "new" ''kuni no miyatsuko''.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'', now in the office of ''gunji'', often sided with peasants against the ruling ''kokushi'' elite. The ''gunji'' position, however, was abolished with the establishment of the manorial ''[[shōen]]'' system in the early [[Heian period]].<ref name="dijitaru-gunji"/> A few ''kuni no miyatsuko'' clans retained influence after the Taika Reforms, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]|出雲国造}} of [[Izumo Province]] in present-day eastern [[Shimane Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) (日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) |title=Izumo Kuni no miyatsuko (出雲国造) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref>
== Legacy == After their abolition several former Kuni no Miyatsuko became prominent priestly families these include the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the [[Owari clan]] of [[Atsuta Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref> the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]<!--original source text from [[Kono Shrine]] *{{nihongo|'''Amabe clan genealogy'''|海部氏系図||}}, early [[Heian period]], it is considered the oldest family tree in Japan.The clan claims descent from Amenohoakari, and served as at the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of [[Tanba Province]] before it was divided into Tamba and Tango.The document records 82 generations of descent from Amenohoakari. It was designated a National Treasure in 1972.--><ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]. {| class="wikitable" |+ !Province !Clan !Shrine |- |[[Izumo Province]] |[[Izumo clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Izumo-taisha]] |- |[[Yamato Province]] |[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] |[[Ōyamato Shrine]] |- |[[Higo Province]]? |[[Aso clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Aso Shrine]] |- |[[Owari Province]] |[[Owari clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Atsuta Shrine]] |- |[[Tanba Province]] |[[Amabe clan]]<ref name="Bunka1" /> |[[Kono Shrine]] |- | rowspan="2" |[[Kii Province]] |Ukai family |[[Kamayama Shrine]] |- |{{ill|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} |[[Hinokuma Shrine]] |}

''Kuni no miyatsuko'' governed provinces called ''kuni'' (国), although the location, names, and borders of the provinces remain unclear. ''Kuni no miyatsuko'' were appointed by and remained under the jurisdiction of the Yamato Court, but over time the position became hereditary.<ref name="dijitaru">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Dijitaru daijisen |title=国造 (Kuni no miyatsuko) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref><ref name="encyclopedia">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Japan |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref> ''Kuni no miyatsuko'' carried ''[[kabane]]'' titles bestowed by the Yamato Court, commonly [[Kimi (kabane)|''kimi'']] (君) or ''[[atae]]'' (直). Prestigious ''kuni no miyatsuko'' held the title of ''[[Omi (title)|omi]]'' (臣).<ref name="encyclopedia"/>
==Taika Reforms==
The office of ''kuni no miyatsuko'' was abolished in the [[Taika Reform]]s in 645 and the former administrative ''kuni'' (provinces) were formally reorganized under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. The provinces became ruled by new officials called ''kuni no mikotomochi'', or more commonly, [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']].<ref name="encyclopedia"/> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'' continued to be appointed after the Taika Reforms, generally to the office of {{Nihongo|gunji|郡司|}}.<ref name="dijitaru"/> ''Gunji'' were appointed from powerful regional ''kuni no miyatsuko'' families, for life, and the position became hereditary.<ref name="dijitaru-gunji">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Dijitaru daijisen |title=郡司 (Gunji) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'' were now in charge of spiritual and religious affairs, specifically the [[Shinto|Shintō]] rites of each province.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> These religious officials became known as {{Nihongo|shin-kokuzō|新国造|}}, or "new" ''kuni no miyatsuko''.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'', now in the office of ''gunji'', often sided with peasants against the ruling ''kokushi'' elite. The ''gunji'' position, however, was abolished with the establishment of the manorial ''[[shōen]]'' system in the early [[Heian period]].<ref name="dijitaru-gunji"/> A few ''kuni no miyatsuko'' clans retained influence after the Taika Reforms, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]|出雲国造}} of [[Izumo Province]] in present-day eastern [[Shimane Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) (日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) |title=Izumo Kuni no miyatsuko (出雲国造) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref>
== Legacy == After their abolition several former Kuni no Miyatsuko became prominent priestly families these include the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the [[Owari clan]] of [[Atsuta Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref> the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]<!--original source text from [[Kono Shrine]] *{{nihongo|'''Amabe clan genealogy'''|海部氏系図||}}, early [[Heian period]], it is considered the oldest family tree in Japan.The clan claims descent from Amenohoakari, and served as at the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of [[Tanba Province]] before it was divided into Tamba and Tango.The document records 82 generations of descent from Amenohoakari. It was designated a National Treasure in 1972.--><ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]. {| class="wikitable" |+ !Province !Clan !Shrine |- |[[Izumo Province]] |[[Izumo clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Izumo-taisha]] |- |[[Yamato Province]] |[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] |[[Ōyamato Shrine]] |- |[[Higo Province]]? |[[Aso clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Aso Shrine]] |- |[[Owari Province]] |[[Owari clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Atsuta Shrine]] |- |[[Tanba Province]] |[[Amabe clan]]<ref name="Bunka1" /> |[[Kono Shrine]] |- | rowspan="2" |[[Kii Province]] |Ukai family |[[Kamayama Shrine]] |- |{{ill|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} |[[Hinokuma Shrine]] |}

{{Short description|Officials in ancient Japan during the Yamato period}} {{Nihongo|'''''Kuni no miyatsuko'''''|国造|}}, also read as '''''kokuzō''''' or '''''kunitsuko''''', were officials in [[ancient Japan]] during the [[Yamato period]] who governed provinces called ''kuni''.
==Yamato period==
''Kuni no miyatsuko'' governed provinces called ''kuni'' (国), although the location, names, and borders of the provinces remain unclear. ''Kuni no miyatsuko'' were appointed by and remained under the jurisdiction of the Yamato Court, but over time the position became hereditary.<ref name="dijitaru">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Dijitaru daijisen |title=国造 (Kuni no miyatsuko) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref><ref name="encyclopedia">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Japan |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref> ''Kuni no miyatsuko'' carried ''[[kabane]]'' titles bestowed by the Yamato Court, commonly [[Kimi (kabane)|''kimi'']] (君) or ''[[atae]]'' (直). Prestigious ''kuni no miyatsuko'' held the title of ''[[Omi (title)|omi]]'' (臣).<ref name="encyclopedia"/>
==Taika Reforms==
The office of ''kuni no miyatsuko'' was abolished in the [[Taika Reform]]s in 645 and the former administrative ''kuni'' (provinces) were formally reorganized under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. The provinces became ruled by new officials called ''kuni no mikotomochi'', or more commonly, [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']].<ref name="encyclopedia"/> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'' continued to be appointed after the Taika Reforms, generally to the office of {{Nihongo|gunji|郡司|}}.<ref name="dijitaru"/> ''Gunji'' were appointed from powerful regional ''kuni no miyatsuko'' families, for life, and the position became hereditary.<ref name="dijitaru-gunji">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Dijitaru daijisen |title=郡司 (Gunji) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'' were now in charge of spiritual and religious affairs, specifically the [[Shinto|Shintō]] rites of each province.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> These religious officials became known as {{Nihongo|shin-kokuzō|新国造|}}, or "new" ''kuni no miyatsuko''.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'', now in the office of ''gunji'', often sided with peasants against the ruling ''kokushi'' elite. The ''gunji'' position, however, was abolished with the establishment of the manorial ''[[shōen]]'' system in the early [[Heian period]].<ref name="dijitaru-gunji"/> A few ''kuni no miyatsuko'' clans retained influence after the Taika Reforms, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]|出雲国造}} of [[Izumo Province]] in present-day eastern [[Shimane Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) (日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) |title=Izumo Kuni no miyatsuko (出雲国造) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref>
== Legacy == After their abolition several former Kuni no Miyatsuko became prominent priestly families these include the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the [[Owari clan]] of [[Atsuta Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref> the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]<!--original source text from [[Kono Shrine]] *{{nihongo|'''Amabe clan genealogy'''|海部氏系図||}}, early [[Heian period]], it is considered the oldest family tree in Japan.The clan claims descent from Amenohoakari, and served as at the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of [[Tanba Province]] before it was divided into Tamba and Tango.The document records 82 generations of descent from Amenohoakari. It was designated a National Treasure in 1972.--><ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]. {| class="wikitable" |+ !Province !Clan !Shrine |- |[[Izumo Province]] |[[Izumo clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Izumo-taisha]] |- |[[Yamato Province]] |[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] |[[Ōyamato Shrine]] |- |[[Higo Province]]? |[[Aso clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Aso Shrine]] |- |[[Owari Province]] |[[Owari clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Atsuta Shrine]] |- |[[Tanba Province]] |[[Amabe clan]]<ref name="Bunka1" /> |[[Kono Shrine]] |- | rowspan="2" |[[Kii Province]] |Ukai family |[[Kamayama Shrine]] |- |{{ill|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} |[[Hinokuma Shrine]] |}

The provinces became ruled by new officials called ''kuni no mikotomochi'', or more commonly, [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']].<ref name="encyclopedia"/> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'' continued to be appointed after the Taika Reforms, generally to the office of {{Nihongo|gunji|郡司|}}.<ref name="dijitaru"/> ''Gunji'' were appointed from powerful regional ''kuni no miyatsuko'' families, for life, and the position became hereditary.<ref name="dijitaru-gunji">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Dijitaru daijisen |title=郡司 (Gunji) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'' were now in charge of spiritual and religious affairs, specifically the [[Shinto|Shintō]] rites of each province.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> These religious officials became known as {{Nihongo|shin-kokuzō|新国造|}}, or "new" ''kuni no miyatsuko''.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'', now in the office of ''gunji'', often sided with peasants against the ruling ''kokushi'' elite. The ''gunji'' position, however, was abolished with the establishment of the manorial ''[[shōen]]'' system in the early [[Heian period]].<ref name="dijitaru-gunji"/> A few ''kuni no miyatsuko'' clans retained influence after the Taika Reforms, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]|出雲国造}} of [[Izumo Province]] in present-day eastern [[Shimane Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) (日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) |title=Izumo Kuni no miyatsuko (出雲国造) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref>
== Legacy == After their abolition several former Kuni no Miyatsuko became prominent priestly families these include the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the [[Owari clan]] of [[Atsuta Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref> the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]<!--original source text from [[Kono Shrine]] *{{nihongo|'''Amabe clan genealogy'''|海部氏系図||}}, early [[Heian period]], it is considered the oldest family tree in Japan.The clan claims descent from Amenohoakari, and served as at the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of [[Tanba Province]] before it was divided into Tamba and Tango.The document records 82 generations of descent from Amenohoakari. It was designated a National Treasure in 1972.--><ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]. {| class="wikitable" |+ !Province !Clan !Shrine |- |[[Izumo Province]] |[[Izumo clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Izumo-taisha]] |- |[[Yamato Province]] |[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] |[[Ōyamato Shrine]] |- |[[Higo Province]]? |[[Aso clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Aso Shrine]] |- |[[Owari Province]] |[[Owari clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Atsuta Shrine]] |- |[[Tanba Province]] |[[Amabe clan]]<ref name="Bunka1" /> |[[Kono Shrine]] |- | rowspan="2" |[[Kii Province]] |Ukai family |[[Kamayama Shrine]] |- |{{ill|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} |[[Hinokuma Shrine]] |}

{{Short description|Officials in ancient Japan during the Yamato period}} {{Nihongo|'''''Kuni no miyatsuko'''''|国造|}}, also read as '''''kokuzō''''' or '''''kunitsuko''''', were officials in [[ancient Japan]] during the [[Yamato period]] who governed provinces called ''kuni''.
==Yamato period==
''Kuni no miyatsuko'' governed provinces called ''kuni'' (国), although the location, names, and borders of the provinces remain unclear. ''Kuni no miyatsuko'' were appointed by and remained under the jurisdiction of the Yamato Court, but over time the position became hereditary.<ref name="dijitaru">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Dijitaru daijisen |title=国造 (Kuni no miyatsuko) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref><ref name="encyclopedia">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Japan |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref> ''Kuni no miyatsuko'' carried ''[[kabane]]'' titles bestowed by the Yamato Court, commonly [[Kimi (kabane)|''kimi'']] (君) or ''[[atae]]'' (直). Prestigious ''kuni no miyatsuko'' held the title of ''[[Omi (title)|omi]]'' (臣).<ref name="encyclopedia"/>
==Taika Reforms==
The office of ''kuni no miyatsuko'' was abolished in the [[Taika Reform]]s in 645 and the former administrative ''kuni'' (provinces) were formally reorganized under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. The provinces became ruled by new officials called ''kuni no mikotomochi'', or more commonly, [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']].<ref name="encyclopedia"/> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'' continued to be appointed after the Taika Reforms, generally to the office of {{Nihongo|gunji|郡司|}}.<ref name="dijitaru"/> ''Gunji'' were appointed from powerful regional ''kuni no miyatsuko'' families, for life, and the position became hereditary.<ref name="dijitaru-gunji">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Dijitaru daijisen |title=郡司 (Gunji) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'' were now in charge of spiritual and religious affairs, specifically the [[Shinto|Shintō]] rites of each province.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> These religious officials became known as {{Nihongo|shin-kokuzō|新国造|}}, or "new" ''kuni no miyatsuko''.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'', now in the office of ''gunji'', often sided with peasants against the ruling ''kokushi'' elite. The ''gunji'' position, however, was abolished with the establishment of the manorial ''[[shōen]]'' system in the early [[Heian period]].<ref name="dijitaru-gunji"/> A few ''kuni no miyatsuko'' clans retained influence after the Taika Reforms, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]|出雲国造}} of [[Izumo Province]] in present-day eastern [[Shimane Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) (日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) |title=Izumo Kuni no miyatsuko (出雲国造) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref>
== Legacy == After their abolition several former Kuni no Miyatsuko became prominent priestly families these include the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the [[Owari clan]] of [[Atsuta Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref> the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]<!--original source text from [[Kono Shrine]] *{{nihongo|'''Amabe clan genealogy'''|海部氏系図||}}, early [[Heian period]], it is considered the oldest family tree in Japan.The clan claims descent from Amenohoakari, and served as at the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of [[Tanba Province]] before it was divided into Tamba and Tango.The document records 82 generations of descent from Amenohoakari. It was designated a National Treasure in 1972.--><ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]. {| class="wikitable" |+ !Province !Clan !Shrine |- |[[Izumo Province]] |[[Izumo clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Izumo-taisha]] |- |[[Yamato Province]] |[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] |[[Ōyamato Shrine]] |- |[[Higo Province]]? |[[Aso clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Aso Shrine]] |- |[[Owari Province]] |[[Owari clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Atsuta Shrine]] |- |[[Tanba Province]] |[[Amabe clan]]<ref name="Bunka1" /> |[[Kono Shrine]] |- | rowspan="2" |[[Kii Province]] |Ukai family |[[Kamayama Shrine]] |- |{{ill|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} |[[Hinokuma Shrine]] |}

revision.text:
{{Short description|Officials in ancient Japan during the Yamato period}} {{Nihongo|'''''Kuni no miyatsuko'''''|国造|}}, also read as '''''kokuzō''''' or '''''kunitsuko''''', were officials in [[ancient Japan]] during the [[Yamato period]] who governed provinces called ''kuni''.
==Yamato period==
''Kuni no miyatsuko'' governed provinces called ''kuni'' (国), although the location, names, and borders of the provinces remain unclear. ''Kuni no miyatsuko'' were appointed by and remained under the jurisdiction of the Yamato Court, but over time the position became hereditary.<ref name="dijitaru">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Dijitaru daijisen |title=国造 (Kuni no miyatsuko) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref><ref name="encyclopedia">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Japan |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref> ''Kuni no miyatsuko'' carried ''[[kabane]]'' titles bestowed by the Yamato Court, commonly [[Kimi (kabane)|''kimi'']] (君) or ''[[atae]]'' (直). Prestigious ''kuni no miyatsuko'' held the title of ''[[Omi (title)|omi]]'' (臣).<ref name="encyclopedia"/>
==Taika Reforms==
The office of ''kuni no miyatsuko'' was abolished in the [[Taika Reform]]s in 645 and the former administrative ''kuni'' (provinces) were formally reorganized under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. The provinces became ruled by new officials called ''kuni no mikotomochi'', or more commonly, [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']].<ref name="encyclopedia"/> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'' continued to be appointed after the Taika Reforms, generally to the office of {{Nihongo|gunji|郡司|}}.<ref name="dijitaru"/> ''Gunji'' were appointed from powerful regional ''kuni no miyatsuko'' families, for life, and the position became hereditary.<ref name="dijitaru-gunji">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Dijitaru daijisen |title=郡司 (Gunji) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'' were now in charge of spiritual and religious affairs, specifically the [[Shinto|Shintō]] rites of each province.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> These religious officials became known as {{Nihongo|shin-kokuzō|新国造|}}, or "new" ''kuni no miyatsuko''.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'', now in the office of ''gunji'', often sided with peasants against the ruling ''kokushi'' elite. The ''gunji'' position, however, was abolished with the establishment of the manorial ''[[shōen]]'' system in the early [[Heian period]].<ref name="dijitaru-gunji"/> A few ''kuni no miyatsuko'' clans retained influence after the Taika Reforms, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]|Izumo Province 造}} of [[Izumo Province]] in present-day eastern [[Shimane Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) (日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) |title=Izumo Kuni no miyatsuko (Izumo Province 造) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref>
== Legacy == After their abolition several former Kuni no Miyatsuko became prominent priestly families these include the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the [[Owari clan]] of [[Atsuta Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref> the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]<!--original source text from [[Kono Shrine]] *{{nihongo|'''Amabe clan genealogy'''|海部氏系図||}}, early [[Heian period]], it is considered the oldest family tree in Japan.The clan claims descent from Amenohoakari, and served as at the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of [[Tanba Province]] before it was divided into Tamba and Tango.The document records 82 generations of descent from Amenohoakari. It was designated a National Treasure in 1972.--><ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]. {| class="wikitable" |+ !Province !Clan !Shrine |- |[[Izumo Province]] |[[Izumo clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Izumo-taisha]] |- |[[Yamato Province]] |[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] |[[Ōyamato Shrine]] |- |[[Higo Province]]? |[[Aso clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Aso Shrine]] |- |[[Owari Province]] |[[Owari clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Atsuta Shrine]] |- |[[Tanba Province]] |[[Amabe clan]]<ref name="Bunka1" /> |[[Kono Shrine]] |- | rowspan="2" |[[Kii Province]] |Ukai family |[[Kamayama Shrine]] |- |{{ill|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} |[[Hinokuma Shrine]] |}

revision.text:
{{Short description|Officials in ancient Japan during the Yamato period}} {{Nihongo|'''''Kuni no miyatsuko'''''|国造|}}, also read as '''''kokuzō''''' or '''''kunitsuko''''', were officials in [[ancient Japan]] during the [[Yamato period]] who governed provinces called ''kuni''.
==Yamato period==
''Kuni no miyatsuko'' governed provinces called ''kuni'' (国), although the location, names, and borders of the provinces remain unclear. ''Kuni no miyatsuko'' were appointed by and remained under the jurisdiction of the Yamato Court, but over time the position became hereditary.<ref name="dijitaru">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Dijitaru daijisen |title=国造 (Kuni no miyatsuko) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref><ref name="encyclopedia">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Japan |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref> ''Kuni no miyatsuko'' carried ''[[kabane]]'' titles bestowed by the Yamato Court, commonly [[Kimi (kabane)|''kimi'']] (君) or ''[[atae]]'' (直). Prestigious ''kuni no miyatsuko'' held the title of ''[[Omi (title)|omi]]'' (臣).<ref name="encyclopedia"/>
==Taika Reforms==
The office of ''kuni no miyatsuko'' was abolished in the [[Taika Reform]]s in 645 and the former administrative ''kuni'' (provinces) were formally reorganized under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. The provinces became ruled by new officials called ''kuni no mikotomochi'', or more commonly, [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']].<ref name="encyclopedia"/> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'' continued to be appointed after the Taika Reforms, generally to the office of {{Nihongo|gunji|郡司|}}.<ref name="dijitaru"/> ''Gunji'' were appointed from powerful regional ''kuni no miyatsuko'' families, for life, and the position became hereditary.<ref name="dijitaru-gunji">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Dijitaru daijisen |title=郡司 (Gunji) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'' were now in charge of spiritual and religious affairs, specifically the [[Shinto|Shintō]] rites of each province.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> These religious officials became known as {{Nihongo|shin-kokuzō|新国造|}}, or "new" ''kuni no miyatsuko''.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'', now in the office of ''gunji'', often sided with peasants against the ruling ''kokushi'' elite. The ''gunji'' position, however, was abolished with the establishment of the manorial ''[[shōen]]'' system in the early [[Heian period]].<ref name="dijitaru-gunji"/> A few ''kuni no miyatsuko'' clans retained influence after the Taika Reforms, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]|Izumo Province 造}} of [[Izumo Province]] in present-day eastern [[Shimane Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) (日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) |title=Izumo Kuni no miyatsuko (Izumo Province 造) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref>
== Legacy == After their abolition several former Kuni no Miyatsuko became prominent priestly families these include the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the [[Owari clan]] of [[Atsuta Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref> the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]<!--original source text from [[Kono Shrine]] *{{nihongo|'''Amabe clan genealogy'''|海部氏系図||}}, early [[Heian period]], it is considered the oldest family tree in Japan.The clan claims descent from Amenohoakari, and served as at the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of [[Tanba Province]] before it was divided into Tamba and Tango.The document records 82 generations of descent from Amenohoakari. It was designated a National Treasure in 1972.--><ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]. {| class="wikitable" |+ !Province !Clan !Shrine |- |[[Izumo Province]] |[[Izumo clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Izumo-taisha]] |- |[[Yamato Province]] |[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] |[[Ōyamato Shrine]] |- |[[Higo Province]]? |[[Aso clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Aso Shrine]] |- |[[Owari Province]] |[[Owari clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Atsuta Shrine]] |- |[[Tanba Province]] |[[Amabe clan]]<ref name="Bunka1" /> |[[Kono Shrine]] |- | rowspan="2" |[[Kii Province]] |Ukai family |[[Kamayama Shrine]] |- |{{ill|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} |[[Hinokuma Shrine]] |}

== Legacy == After their abolition several former Kuni no Miyatsuko became prominent priestly families these include the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the [[Owari clan]] of [[Atsuta Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref> the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]<!--original source text from [[Kono Shrine]] *{{nihongo|'''Amabe clan genealogy'''|海部氏系図||}}, early [[Heian period]], it is considered the oldest family tree in Japan.The clan claims descent from Amenohoakari, and served as at the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of [[Tanba Province]] before it was divided into Tamba and Tango.The document records 82 generations of descent from Amenohoakari. It was designated a National Treasure in 1972.--><ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]. {| class="wikitable" |+ !Province !Clan !Shrine |- |[[Izumo Province]] |[[Izumo clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Izumo-taisha]] |- |[[Yamato Province]] |[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] |[[Ōyamato Shrine]] |- |[[Higo Province]]? |[[Aso clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Aso Shrine]] |- |[[Owari Province]] |[[Owari clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Atsuta Shrine]] |- |[[Tanba Province]] |[[Amabe clan]]<ref name="Bunka1" /> |[[Kono Shrine]] |- | rowspan="2" |[[Kii Province]] |Ukai family |[[Kamayama Shrine]] |- |{{ill|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} |[[Hinokuma Shrine]] |}

{{Short description|Officials in ancient Japan during the Yamato period}} {{Nihongo|'''''Kuni no miyatsuko'''''|国造|}}, also read as '''''kokuzō''''' or '''''kunitsuko''''', were officials in [[ancient Japan]] during the [[Yamato period]] who governed provinces called ''kuni''.
==Yamato period==
''Kuni no miyatsuko'' governed provinces called ''kuni'' (国), although the location, names, and borders of the provinces remain unclear. ''Kuni no miyatsuko'' were appointed by and remained under the jurisdiction of the Yamato Court, but over time the position became hereditary.<ref name="dijitaru">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Dijitaru daijisen |title=国造 (Kuni no miyatsuko) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref><ref name="encyclopedia">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Japan |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref> ''Kuni no miyatsuko'' carried ''[[kabane]]'' titles bestowed by the Yamato Court, commonly [[Kimi (kabane)|''kimi'']] (君) or ''[[atae]]'' (直). Prestigious ''kuni no miyatsuko'' held the title of ''[[Omi (title)|omi]]'' (臣).<ref name="encyclopedia"/>
==Taika Reforms==
The office of ''kuni no miyatsuko'' was abolished in the [[Taika Reform]]s in 645 and the former administrative ''kuni'' (provinces) were formally reorganized under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. The provinces became ruled by new officials called ''kuni no mikotomochi'', or more commonly, [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']].<ref name="encyclopedia"/> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'' continued to be appointed after the Taika Reforms, generally to the office of {{Nihongo|gunji|郡司|}}.<ref name="dijitaru"/> ''Gunji'' were appointed from powerful regional ''kuni no miyatsuko'' families, for life, and the position became hereditary.<ref name="dijitaru-gunji">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Dijitaru daijisen |title=郡司 (Gunji) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'' were now in charge of spiritual and religious affairs, specifically the [[Shinto|Shintō]] rites of each province.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> These religious officials became known as {{Nihongo|shin-kokuzō|新国造|}}, or "new" ''kuni no miyatsuko''.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'', now in the office of ''gunji'', often sided with peasants against the ruling ''kokushi'' elite. The ''gunji'' position, however, was abolished with the establishment of the manorial ''[[shōen]]'' system in the early [[Heian period]].<ref name="dijitaru-gunji"/> A few ''kuni no miyatsuko'' clans retained influence after the Taika Reforms, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]|Izumo Province 造}} of [[Izumo Province]] in present-day eastern [[Shimane Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) (日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) |title=Izumo Kuni no miyatsuko (Izumo Province 造) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref>
== Legacy == After their abolition several former Kuni no Miyatsuko became prominent priestly families these include the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the [[Owari clan]] of [[Atsuta Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref> the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]<!--original source text from [[Kono Shrine]] *{{nihongo|'''Amabe clan genealogy'''|海部氏系図||}}, early [[Heian period]], it is considered the oldest family tree in Japan.The clan claims descent from Amenohoakari, and served as at the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of [[Tanba Province]] before it was divided into Tamba and Tango.The document records 82 generations of descent from Amenohoakari. It was designated a National Treasure in 1972.--><ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]. {| class="wikitable" |+ !Province !Clan !Shrine |- |[[Izumo Province]] |[[Izumo clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Izumo-taisha]] |- |[[Yamato Province]] |[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] |[[Ōyamato Shrine]] |- |[[Higo Province]]? |[[Aso clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Aso Shrine]] |- |[[Owari Province]] |[[Owari clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Atsuta Shrine]] |- |[[Tanba Province]] |[[Amabe clan]]<ref name="Bunka1" /> |[[Kono Shrine]] |- | rowspan="2" |[[Kii Province]] |Ukai family |[[Kamayama Shrine]] |- |{{ill|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀伊国造}} |[[Hinokuma Shrine]] |}

revision.text:
{{Short description|Officials in ancient Japan during the Yamato period}} {{Nihongo|'''''Kuni no miyatsuko'''''|国造|}}, also read as '''''kokuzō''''' or '''''kunitsuko''''', were officials in [[ancient Japan]] during the [[Yamato period]] who governed provinces called ''kuni''.
==Yamato period==
''Kuni no miyatsuko'' governed provinces called ''kuni'' (国), although the location, names, and borders of the provinces remain unclear. ''Kuni no miyatsuko'' were appointed by and remained under the jurisdiction of the Yamato Court, but over time the position became hereditary.<ref name="dijitaru">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Dijitaru daijisen |title=国造 (Kuni no miyatsuko) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref><ref name="encyclopedia">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Japan |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref> ''Kuni no miyatsuko'' carried ''[[kabane]]'' titles bestowed by the Yamato Court, commonly [[Kimi (kabane)|''kimi'']] (君) or ''[[atae]]'' (直). Prestigious ''kuni no miyatsuko'' held the title of ''[[Omi (title)|omi]]'' (臣).<ref name="encyclopedia"/>
==Taika Reforms==
The office of ''kuni no miyatsuko'' was abolished in the [[Taika Reform]]s in 645 and the former administrative ''kuni'' (provinces) were formally reorganized under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. The provinces became ruled by new officials called ''kuni no mikotomochi'', or more commonly, [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']].<ref name="encyclopedia"/> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'' continued to be appointed after the Taika Reforms, generally to the office of {{Nihongo|gunji|郡司|}}.<ref name="dijitaru"/> ''Gunji'' were appointed from powerful regional ''kuni no miyatsuko'' families, for life, and the position became hereditary.<ref name="dijitaru-gunji">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Dijitaru daijisen |title=郡司 (Gunji) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'' were now in charge of spiritual and religious affairs, specifically the [[Shinto|Shintō]] rites of each province.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> These religious officials became known as {{Nihongo|shin-kokuzō|新国造|}}, or "new" ''kuni no miyatsuko''.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'', now in the office of ''gunji'', often sided with peasants against the ruling ''kokushi'' elite. The ''gunji'' position, however, was abolished with the establishment of the manorial ''[[shōen]]'' system in the early [[Heian period]].<ref name="dijitaru-gunji"/> A few ''kuni no miyatsuko'' clans retained influence after the Taika Reforms, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]|Izumo Province 造}} of [[Izumo Province]] in present-day eastern [[Shimane Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) (日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) |title=Izumo Kuni no miyatsuko (Izumo Province 造) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref>
== Legacy == After their abolition several former Kuni no Miyatsuko became prominent priestly families these include the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the [[Owari clan]] of [[Atsuta Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref> the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]<!--original source text from [[Kono Shrine]] *{{nihongo|'''Amabe clan genealogy'''|海部氏系図||}}, early [[Heian period]], it is considered the oldest family tree in Japan.The clan claims descent from Amenohoakari, and served as at the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of [[Tanba Province]] before it was divided into Tamba and Tango.The document records 82 generations of descent from Amenohoakari. It was designated a National Treasure in 1972.--><ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]. {| class="wikitable" |+ !Province !Clan !Shrine |- |[[Izumo Province]] |[[Izumo clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Izumo-taisha]] |- |[[Yamato Province]] |[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] |[[Ōyamato Shrine]] |- |[[Higo Province]]? |[[Aso clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Aso Shrine]] |- |[[Owari Province]] |[[Owari clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Atsuta Shrine]] |- |[[Tanba Province]] |[[Amabe clan]]<ref name="Bunka1" /> |[[Kono Shrine]] |- | rowspan="2" |[[Kii Province]] |Ukai family |[[Kamayama Shrine]] |- |{{ill|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀氏|1=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|de|Ki (Klan)|uk|Рід Кі|ko|기씨 (황별)|fr|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|3=紀伊国造}} |[[Hinokuma Shrine]] |}

The ''gunji'' position, however, was abolished with the establishment of the manorial ''[[shōen]]'' system in the early [[Heian period]].<ref name="dijitaru-gunji"/> A few ''kuni no miyatsuko'' clans retained influence after the Taika Reforms, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]|Izumo Province 造}} of [[Izumo Province]] in present-day eastern [[Shimane Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) (日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) |title=Izumo Kuni no miyatsuko (Izumo Province 造) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref>
== Legacy == After their abolition several former Kuni no Miyatsuko became prominent priestly families these include the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the [[Owari clan]] of [[Atsuta Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref> the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]<!--original source text from [[Kono Shrine]] *{{nihongo|'''Amabe clan genealogy'''|海部氏系図||}}, early [[Heian period]], it is considered the oldest family tree in Japan.The clan claims descent from Amenohoakari, and served as at the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of [[Tanba Province]] before it was divided into Tamba and Tango.The document records 82 generations of descent from Amenohoakari. It was designated a National Treasure in 1972.--><ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]. {| class="wikitable" |+ !Province !Clan !Shrine |- |[[Izumo Province]] |[[Izumo clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Izumo-taisha]] |- |[[Yamato Province]] |[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] |[[Ōyamato Shrine]] |- |[[Higo Province]]? |[[Aso clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Aso Shrine]] |- |[[Owari Province]] |[[Owari clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Atsuta Shrine]] |- |[[Tanba Province]] |[[Amabe clan]]<ref name="Bunka1" /> |[[Kono Shrine]] |- | rowspan="2" |[[Kii Province]] |Ukai family |[[Kamayama Shrine]] |- |{{ill|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀氏|1=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|de|Ki (Klan)|uk|Рід Кі|ko|기씨 (황별)|fr|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|3=紀伊国造}} |[[Hinokuma Shrine]] |}

revision.text:
{{Short description|Officials in ancient Japan during the Yamato period}} {{Nihongo|'''''Kuni no miyatsuko'''''|国造|}}, also read as '''''kokuzō''''' or '''''kunitsuko''''', were officials in [[ancient Japan]] during the [[Yamato period]] who governed provinces called ''kuni''.
==Yamato period==
''Kuni no miyatsuko'' governed provinces called ''kuni'' (国), although the location, names, and borders of the provinces remain unclear. ''Kuni no miyatsuko'' were appointed by and remained under the jurisdiction of the Yamato Court, but over time the position became hereditary.<ref name="dijitaru">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Dijitaru daijisen |title=国造 (Kuni no miyatsuko) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref><ref name="encyclopedia">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Japan |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref> ''Kuni no miyatsuko'' carried ''[[kabane]]'' titles bestowed by the Yamato Court, commonly [[Kimi (kabane)|''kimi'']] (君) or ''[[atae]]'' (直). Prestigious ''kuni no miyatsuko'' held the title of ''[[Omi (title)|omi]]'' (臣).<ref name="encyclopedia"/>
==Taika Reforms==
The office of ''kuni no miyatsuko'' was abolished in the [[Taika Reform]]s in 645 and the former administrative ''kuni'' (provinces) were formally reorganized under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. The provinces became ruled by new officials called ''kuni no mikotomochi'', or more commonly, [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']].<ref name="encyclopedia"/> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'' continued to be appointed after the Taika Reforms, generally to the office of {{Nihongo|gunji|郡司|}}.<ref name="dijitaru"/> ''Gunji'' were appointed from powerful regional ''kuni no miyatsuko'' families, for life, and the position became hereditary.<ref name="dijitaru-gunji">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Dijitaru daijisen |title=郡司 (Gunji) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'' were now in charge of spiritual and religious affairs, specifically the [[Shinto|Shintō]] rites of each province.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> These religious officials became known as {{Nihongo|shin-kokuzō|新国造|}}, or "new" ''kuni no miyatsuko''.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'', now in the office of ''gunji'', often sided with peasants against the ruling ''kokushi'' elite. The ''gunji'' position, however, was abolished with the establishment of the manorial ''[[shōen]]'' system in the early [[Heian period]].<ref name="dijitaru-gunji"/> A few ''kuni no miyatsuko'' clans retained influence after the Taika Reforms, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]|Izumo Province 造}} of [[Izumo Province]] in present-day eastern [[Shimane Prefecture]].<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) (日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) |title=Izumo Kuni no miyatsuko (Izumo Province 造) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref>
== Legacy == After their abolition several former Kuni no Miyatsuko became prominent priestly families these include the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the [[Owari clan]] of [[Atsuta Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref> the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]<!--original source text from [[Kono Shrine]] *{{nihongo|'''Amabe clan genealogy'''|海部氏系図||}}, early [[Heian period]], it is considered the oldest family tree in Japan.The clan claims descent from Amenohoakari, and served as at the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of [[Tanba Province]] before it was divided into Tamba and Tango.The document records 82 generations of descent from Amenohoakari. It was designated a National Treasure in 1972.--><ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]. {| class="wikitable" |+ !Province !Clan !Shrine |- |[[Izumo Province]] |[[Izumo clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Izumo-taisha]] |- |[[Yamato Province]] |[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] |[[Ōyamato Shrine]] |- |[[Higo Province]]? |[[Aso clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Aso Shrine]] |- |[[Owari Province]] |[[Owari clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Atsuta Shrine]] |- |[[Tanba Province]] |[[Amabe clan]]<ref name="Bunka1" /> |[[Kono Shrine]] |- | rowspan="2" |[[Kii Province]] |Ukai family |[[Kamayama Shrine]] |- |{{ill|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀氏|1=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|de|Ki (Klan)|uk|Рід Кі|ko|기씨 (황별)|fr|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|3=紀伊国造}} |[[Hinokuma Shrine]] |}
