{{Short description|Officials in ancient Japan during the Yamato period}} {{Nihongo|'''''Kuni no miyatsuko'''''|国造|}}, also read as '''''kokuzō''''' or '''''kunitsuko''''', were officials in [[:en:Ancient Japan|Ancient Japan]] during the {{ill|Yamato period|en|Yamato period|WD=Q633881|lt=Yamato period}} who governed provinces called ''kuni''.
==Yamato period==
''Kuni no miyatsuko'' governed provinces called ''kuni'' (国), although the location, names, and borders of the provinces remain unclear. ''Kuni no miyatsuko'' were appointed by and remained under the jurisdiction of the Yamato Court, but over time the position became hereditary.<ref name="dijitaru">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Dijitaru daijisen |title=国造 (Kuni no miyatsuko) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref><ref name="encyclopedia">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Japan |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref> ''Kuni no miyatsuko'' carried ''[[kabane]]'' titles bestowed by the Yamato Court, commonly [[Kimi (kabane)|''kimi'']] (君) or ''[[atae]]'' (直). Prestigious ''kuni no miyatsuko'' held the title of ''[[Omi (title)|omi]]'' (臣).<ref name="encyclopedia"/>
==Taika Reforms==
The office of ''kuni no miyatsuko'' was abolished in the [[Taika Reform]]s in 645 and the former administrative ''kuni'' (provinces) were formally reorganized under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. The provinces became ruled by new officials called ''kuni no mikotomochi'', or more commonly, {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|WD=Q1351234|lt=''kokushi''}}.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'' continued to be appointed after the Taika Reforms, generally to the office of {{Nihongo|gunji|郡司|}}.<ref name="dijitaru"/> ''Gunji'' were appointed from powerful regional ''kuni no miyatsuko'' families, for life, and the position became hereditary.<ref name="dijitaru-gunji">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Dijitaru daijisen |title=郡司 (Gunji) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'' were now in charge of spiritual and religious affairs, specifically the [[Shinto|Shintō]] rites of each province.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> These religious officials became known as {{Nihongo|shin-kokuzō|新国造|}}, or "new" ''kuni no miyatsuko''.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'', now in the office of ''gunji'', often sided with peasants against the ruling ''kokushi'' elite. The ''gunji'' position, however, was abolished with the establishment of the manorial ''[[shōen]]'' system in the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}.<ref name="dijitaru-gunji"/> A few ''kuni no miyatsuko'' clans retained influence after the Taika Reforms, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]|Izumo Province 造}} of {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo Province}} in present-day eastern {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|WD=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture}}.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) (日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) |title=Izumo Kuni no miyatsuko (Izumo Province 造) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref>
== Legacy == After their abolition several former Kuni no Miyatsuko became prominent priestly families these include the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the [[Owari clan]] of [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|qq=}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref> the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]. {| class="wikitable" |+ !Province !Clan !Shrine |- |{{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo Province}} |[[Izumo clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Izumo-taisha]] |- |{{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province}} |[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] |[[Ōyamato Shrine]] |- |{{ill|Higo Province|en|Higo Province|WD=Q259705|lt=Higo Province}}? <!--original source text from [[Kono Shrine]] *{{nihongo|'''Amabe clan genealogy'''|海部氏系図||}}, early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, it is considered the oldest family tree in Japan.The clan claims descent from Amenohoakari, and served as at the ''[[kuni no miyatsuko]]'' of {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|WD=Q954511|lt=Tanba Province|1=Tanba Province}} before it was divided into Tanba and Tango.The document records 82 generations of descent from Amenohoakari. It was designated a National Treasure in 1972.--> <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[es:Kuni no miyatsuko]] [[it:Kuni no miyatsuko]] [[ja:国造]] [[ko:국조 (일본)]] [[simple:Kuni no miyatsuko]] [[vi:Kuni no miyatsuko]] [[zh:國造]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kuni no miyatsuko]][[es:Kuni no miyatsuko]][[ff:Kuni no miyatsuko]][[it:Kuni no miyatsuko]][[ja:国造]][[ko:국조 (일본)]][[simple:Kuni no miyatsuko]][[vi:Kuni no miyatsuko]][[zh:國造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kuni no miyatsuko]][[es:Kuni no miyatsuko]][[ff:Kuni no miyatsuko]][[it:Kuni no miyatsuko]][[ja:国造]][[ko:국조 (일본)]][[simple:Kuni no miyatsuko]][[vi:Kuni no miyatsuko]][[zh:國造]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kuni no miyatsuko]][[es:Kuni no miyatsuko]][[ff:Kuni no miyatsuko]][[it:Kuni no miyatsuko]][[ja:国造]][[ko:국조 (일본)]][[simple:Kuni no miyatsuko]][[vi:Kuni no miyatsuko]][[zh:國造]]
{{Short description|Officials in ancient Japan during the Yamato period}} {{Nihongo|'''''Kuni no miyatsuko'''''|国造|}}, also read as '''''kokuzō''''' or '''''kunitsuko''''', were officials in [[:en:Ancient Japan|Ancient Japan]] during the {{ill|Yamato period|en|Yamato period|WD=Q633881|lt=Yamato period|1=Yamato period}} who governed provinces called ''kuni''.
==Yamato period==
''Kuni no miyatsuko'' governed provinces called ''kuni'' (国), although the location, names, and borders of the provinces remain unclear. ''Kuni no miyatsuko'' were appointed by and remained under the jurisdiction of the Yamato Court, but over time the position became hereditary.<ref name="dijitaru">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Dijitaru daijisen |title=国造 (Kuni no miyatsuko) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref><ref name="encyclopedia">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Japan |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref> ''Kuni no miyatsuko'' carried ''[[kabane]]'' titles bestowed by the Yamato Court, commonly [[Kimi (kabane)|''kimi'']] (君) or ''[[atae]]'' (直). Prestigious ''kuni no miyatsuko'' held the title of ''[[Omi (title)|omi]]'' (臣).<ref name="encyclopedia"/>
==Taika Reforms==
The office of ''kuni no miyatsuko'' was abolished in the [[Taika Reform]]s in 645 and the former administrative ''kuni'' (provinces) were formally reorganized under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. The provinces became ruled by new officials called ''kuni no mikotomochi'', or more commonly, {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|WD=Q1351234|lt=''kokushi''|1=Kokushi (official)}}.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'' continued to be appointed after the Taika Reforms, generally to the office of {{Nihongo|gunji|郡司|}}.<ref name="dijitaru"/> ''Gunji'' were appointed from powerful regional ''kuni no miyatsuko'' families, for life, and the position became hereditary.<ref name="dijitaru-gunji">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Dijitaru daijisen |title=郡司 (Gunji) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'' were now in charge of spiritual and religious affairs, specifically the [[Shinto|Shintō]] rites of each province.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> These religious officials became known as {{Nihongo|shin-kokuzō|新国造|}}, or "new" ''kuni no miyatsuko''.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'', now in the office of ''gunji'', often sided with peasants against the ruling ''kokushi'' elite. The ''gunji'' position, however, was abolished with the establishment of the manorial ''[[shōen]]'' system in the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.<ref name="dijitaru-gunji"/> A few ''kuni no miyatsuko'' clans retained influence after the Taika Reforms, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]|Izumo Province 造}} of {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo Province|1=Izumo Province}} in present-day eastern {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|WD=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture|1=Shimane Prefecture}}.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) (日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) |title=Izumo Kuni no miyatsuko (Izumo Province 造) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref>
== Legacy == After their abolition several former Kuni no Miyatsuko became prominent priestly families these include the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the [[Owari clan]] of [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|qq=}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref> the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]. {| class="wikitable" |+ !Province !Clan !Shrine |- |{{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo Province|1=Izumo Province}} |[[Izumo clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Izumo-taisha]] |- |{{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}} |[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] |[[Ōyamato Shrine]] |- |{{ill|Higo Province|en|Higo Province|WD=Q259705|lt=Higo Province|1=Higo Province}}? |[[Aso clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Aso Shrine]] |- |{{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari Province|1=Owari Province}} |[[Owari clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] |- |{{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|WD=Q954511|lt=Tanba Province|1=Tanba Province}} |[[Amabe clan]]<ref name="Bunka1" /> |[[Kono Shrine]] |- | rowspan="2" |{{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province|1=Kii Province}} |Ukai family |[[Kamayama Shrine]] |- |{{ill|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀氏|1=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|de|Ki (Klan)|uk|Рід Кі|ko|기씨 (황별)|fr|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|3=紀伊国造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}} |[[Hinokuma Shrine]] |}

It was designated a National Treasure in 1972.--> <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[es:Kuni no miyatsuko]] [[it:Kuni no miyatsuko]] [[ja:国造]] [[ko:국조 (일본)]] [[simple:Kuni no miyatsuko]] [[vi:Kuni no miyatsuko]] [[zh:國造]]

Prestigious ''kuni no miyatsuko'' held the title of ''[[Omi (title)|omi]]'' (臣).<ref name="encyclopedia"/>

The office of ''kuni no miyatsuko'' was abolished in the [[Taika Reform]]s in 645 and the former administrative ''kuni'' (provinces) were formally reorganized under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. |[[Aso clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Aso Shrine]] |- |{{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari Province|1=Owari Province}} |[[Owari clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] |- |{{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|WD=Q954511|lt=Tanba Province|1=Tanba Province}} |[[Amabe clan]]<ref name="Bunka1" /> |[[Kono Shrine]] |- | rowspan="2" |{{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province|1=Kii Province}} |Ukai family |[[Kamayama Shrine]] |- |{{ill|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀氏|1=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|de|Ki (Klan)|uk|Рід Кі|ko|기씨 (황별)|fr|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|3=紀伊国造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}} |[[Hinokuma Shrine]] |}

==Taika Reforms==
The office of ''kuni no miyatsuko'' was abolished in the [[Taika Reform]]s in 645 and the former administrative ''kuni'' (provinces) were formally reorganized under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. The provinces became ruled by new officials called ''kuni no mikotomochi'', or more commonly, {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|WD=Q1351234|lt=''kokushi''|1=Kokushi (official)}}.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'' continued to be appointed after the Taika Reforms, generally to the office of {{Nihongo|gunji|郡司|}}.<ref name="dijitaru"/> ''Gunji'' were appointed from powerful regional ''kuni no miyatsuko'' families, for life, and the position became hereditary.<ref name="dijitaru-gunji">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Dijitaru daijisen |title=郡司 (Gunji) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'' were now in charge of spiritual and religious affairs, specifically the [[Shinto|Shintō]] rites of each province.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> These religious officials became known as {{Nihongo|shin-kokuzō|新国造|}}, or "new" ''kuni no miyatsuko''.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'', now in the office of ''gunji'', often sided with peasants against the ruling ''kokushi'' elite. The ''gunji'' position, however, was abolished with the establishment of the manorial ''[[shōen]]'' system in the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.<ref name="dijitaru-gunji"/> A few ''kuni no miyatsuko'' clans retained influence after the Taika Reforms, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]|Izumo Province 造}} of {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo Province|1=Izumo Province}} in present-day eastern {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|WD=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture|1=Shimane Prefecture}}.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) (日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) |title=Izumo Kuni no miyatsuko (Izumo Province 造) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref>
== Legacy == After their abolition several former Kuni no Miyatsuko became prominent priestly families these include the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the [[Owari clan]] of [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|qq=}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref> the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]. {| class="wikitable" |+ !Province !Clan !Shrine |- |{{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo Province|1=Izumo Province}} |[[Izumo clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Izumo-taisha]] |- |{{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}} |[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] |[[Ōyamato Shrine]] |- |{{ill|Higo Province|en|Higo Province|WD=Q259705|lt=Higo Province|1=Higo Province}}? |[[Aso clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Aso Shrine]] |- |{{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari Province|1=Owari Province}} |[[Owari clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] |- |{{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|WD=Q954511|lt=Tanba Province|1=Tanba Province}} |[[Amabe clan]]<ref name="Bunka1" /> |[[Kono Shrine]] |- | rowspan="2" |{{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province|1=Kii Province}} |Ukai family |[[Kamayama Shrine]] |- |{{ill|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀氏|1=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|de|Ki (Klan)|uk|Рід Кі|ko|기씨 (황별)|fr|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|3=紀伊国造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}} |[[Hinokuma Shrine]] |}

It was designated a National Treasure in 1972.--> <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[es:Kuni no miyatsuko]] [[it:Kuni no miyatsuko]] [[ja:国造]] [[ko:국조 (일본)]] [[simple:Kuni no miyatsuko]] [[vi:Kuni no miyatsuko]] [[zh:國造]]

{{Short description|Officials in ancient Japan during the Yamato period}} {{Nihongo|'''''Kuni no miyatsuko'''''|国造|}}, also read as '''''kokuzō''''' or '''''kunitsuko''''', were officials in [[:en:Ancient Japan|Ancient Japan]] during the {{ill|Yamato period|en|Yamato period|WD=Q633881|lt=Yamato period|1=Yamato period}} who governed provinces called ''kuni''.
==Yamato period==
''Kuni no miyatsuko'' governed provinces called ''kuni'' (国), although the location, names, and borders of the provinces remain unclear. ''Kuni no miyatsuko'' were appointed by and remained under the jurisdiction of the Yamato Court, but over time the position became hereditary.<ref name="dijitaru">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Dijitaru daijisen |title=国造 (Kuni no miyatsuko) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref><ref name="encyclopedia">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Encyclopedia of Japan |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref> ''Kuni no miyatsuko'' carried ''[[kabane]]'' titles bestowed by the Yamato Court, commonly [[Kimi (kabane)|''kimi'']] (君) or ''[[atae]]'' (直). Prestigious ''kuni no miyatsuko'' held the title of ''[[Omi (title)|omi]]'' (臣).<ref name="encyclopedia"/>
==Taika Reforms==
The office of ''kuni no miyatsuko'' was abolished in the [[Taika Reform]]s in 645 and the former administrative ''kuni'' (provinces) were formally reorganized under the ''[[Ritsuryō]]'' system. The provinces became ruled by new officials called ''kuni no mikotomochi'', or more commonly, {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|WD=Q1351234|lt=''kokushi''|1=Kokushi (official)}}.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'' continued to be appointed after the Taika Reforms, generally to the office of {{Nihongo|gunji|郡司|}}.<ref name="dijitaru"/> ''Gunji'' were appointed from powerful regional ''kuni no miyatsuko'' families, for life, and the position became hereditary.<ref name="dijitaru-gunji">{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Dijitaru daijisen |title=郡司 (Gunji) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'' were now in charge of spiritual and religious affairs, specifically the [[Shinto|Shintō]] rites of each province.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> These religious officials became known as {{Nihongo|shin-kokuzō|新国造|}}, or "new" ''kuni no miyatsuko''.<ref name="encyclopedia"/> The ''kuni no miyatsuko'', now in the office of ''gunji'', often sided with peasants against the ruling ''kokushi'' elite. The ''gunji'' position, however, was abolished with the establishment of the manorial ''[[shōen]]'' system in the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.<ref name="dijitaru-gunji"/> A few ''kuni no miyatsuko'' clans retained influence after the Taika Reforms, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]|Izumo Province 造}} of {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo Province|1=Izumo Province}} in present-day eastern {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|WD=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture|1=Shimane Prefecture}}.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) (日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) |title=Izumo Kuni no miyatsuko (Izumo Province 造) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref>
== Legacy == After their abolition several former Kuni no Miyatsuko became prominent priestly families these include the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the [[Owari clan]] of [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|qq=}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref> the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]. {| class="wikitable" |+ !Province !Clan !Shrine |- |{{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo Province|1=Izumo Province}} |[[Izumo clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Izumo-taisha]] |- |{{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}} |[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] |[[Ōyamato Shrine]] |- |{{ill|Higo Province|en|Higo Province|WD=Q259705|lt=Higo Province|1=Higo Province}}? |[[Aso clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Aso Shrine]] |- |{{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari Province|1=Owari Province}} |[[Owari clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] |- |{{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|WD=Q954511|lt=Tanba Province|1=Tanba Province}} |[[Amabe clan]]<ref name="Bunka1" /> |[[Kono Shrine]] |- | rowspan="2" |{{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province|1=Kii Province}} |Ukai family |[[Kamayama Shrine]] |- |{{ill|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀氏|1=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|de|Ki (Klan)|uk|Рід Кі|ko|기씨 (황별)|fr|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|3=紀伊国造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}} |[[Hinokuma Shrine]] |}

It was designated a National Treasure in 1972.--> <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[es:Kuni no miyatsuko]] [[it:Kuni no miyatsuko]] [[ja:国造]] [[ko:국조 (일본)]] [[simple:Kuni no miyatsuko]] [[vi:Kuni no miyatsuko]] [[zh:國造]]

The ''gunji'' position, however, was abolished with the establishment of the manorial ''[[shōen]]'' system in the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.<ref name="dijitaru-gunji"/> A few ''kuni no miyatsuko'' clans retained influence after the Taika Reforms, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo clan|Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]|Izumo Province 造}} of {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|qid=Q582846|lt=Izumo Province|1=Izumo Province}} in present-day eastern {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|qid=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture|1=Shimane Prefecture}}.<ref>{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Nihon Daihyakka Zensho (Nipponika) (日本大百科全書(ニッポニカ) |title=Izumo Kuni no miyatsuko (Izumo Province 造) |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-04-12 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=ja |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }}</ref>
== Legacy == After their abolition several former Kuni no Miyatsuko became prominent priestly families these include the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the [[Owari clan]] of [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|qq=}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref> the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |title=海部氏系図 |trans-title=Amebe shikeizu |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777 |accessdate=August 20, 2020 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |language=Japanese}}</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]. {| class="wikitable" |+ !Province !Clan !Shrine |- |{{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|qid=Q582846|lt=Izumo Province|1=Izumo Province}} |[[Izumo clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Izumo-taisha]] |- |{{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|qid=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}} |[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] |[[Ōyamato Shrine]] |- |{{ill|Higo Province|en|Higo Province|qid=Q259705|lt=Higo Province|1=Higo Province}}? |[[Aso clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[Aso Shrine]] |- |{{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|qid=Q514506|lt=Owari Province|1=Owari Province}} |[[Owari clan]]<ref name=":1" /> |[[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] |- |{{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|qid=Q954511|lt=Tanba Province|1=Tanba Province}} |[[Amabe clan]]<ref name="Bunka1" /> |[[Kono Shrine]] |- | rowspan="2" |{{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|qid=Q864053|lt=Kii Province|1=Kii Province}} |Ukai family |[[Kamayama Shrine]] |- |{{ill|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|紀氏|1=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|de|Ki (Klan)|uk|Рід Кі|ko|기씨 (황별)|fr|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|3=紀伊国造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}} |[[Hinokuma Shrine]] |}
